title: LLM Engineering Deep Dive
description: [Intuition]: LLM engineering bridges the gap between training large language models and deploying them reliably at scale. This file covers prompt engineering patterns, RAG architecture, fine-tuning, alignment, safety, evaluation, agents, serving, and context optimization — from first principles to FAANG production best practices.
details: [Objectives]: Master the end-to-end LLM lifecycle — prompting, retrieval-augmented generation, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, RLHF alignment, safety evaluation, agent architectures, and production serving. Each section builds toward Staff+ level depth expected at FAANG companies in 2026.

sections.title: Prompt Engineering Patterns
sections.description:
[Intuition]: Prompt engineering is the art of steering a frozen LLM toward desired outputs without modifying weights. The key insight is that LLMs learn to follow patterns they see in-context — the prompt is a program written in natural language that the model executes during inference. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) works because it converts a direct computation into a sequence of simpler steps that reduces error accumulation. Each pattern has a specific failure mode: zero-shot can miss domain context, few-shot can overfit to exemplar surface forms, CoT can produce plausible-sounding wrong answers. The choice depends on task complexity, available exemplars, and latency budget.

[Concrete Numbers]: GPT-4 with Chain-of-Thought achieves 97.6% on GSM8K (grade school math) vs 63.2% without CoT — a 34.4 point improvement from a single prompt instruction. Few-shot with 5 exemplars typically matches or beats zero-shot with 500 fine-tuning examples on classification tasks, making prompting 100x more sample-efficient than fine-tuning for some tasks. CoT with self-consistency (k=10 samples, majority vote) boosts accuracy to 99.1% on GSM8K.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Start with zero-shot, 2) If failing, add 3-5 exemplars (few-shot), 3) For reasoning tasks, add "Let's think step by step" (CoT), 4) For high-stakes, use self-consistency (sample multiple CoT paths, vote), 5) Measure: accuracy, token cost, latency per pattern.

[Interview Angle]: Google L6 | Tests whether you understand prompting as a systems design problem (latency-cost-accuracy Pareto frontier) rather than a linguistic trick.

[Common Mistakes]: Using few-shot when zero-shot would suffice (wastes context window + tokens). Adding irrelevant exemplars that bias the model. Forgetting to set temperature low (0.0-0.3) for deterministic tasks vs high (0.7-1.0) for creative tasks.
sections.codeBlock:
import openai

# Chain-of-Thought prompt template
SYSTEM_PROMPT = "You are a math tutor. Always show your reasoning step by step."

FEW_SHOT_COT = """
Q: John has 3 apples. He buys 5 more. How many does he have?
A: Let's think step by step.
John starts with 3 apples.
He buys 5 more, so we add: 3 + 5 = 8.
John has 8 apples. Answer: 8

Q: A farmer has 12 chickens. Each chicken lays 2 eggs per day. How many eggs in 7 days?
A: Let's think step by step.
Each chicken lays 2 eggs per day.
12 chickens lay 12 × 2 = 24 eggs per day.
Over 7 days: 24 × 7 = 168 eggs. Answer: 168

Q: {{question}}
A: Let's think step by step.
"""

def chain_of_thought(question, model="gpt-4"):
    prompt = FEW_SHOT_COT.replace("{{question}}", question)
    response = openai.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": prompt}
        ],
        temperature=0.2,  # low temp for deterministic reasoning
        max_tokens=512
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

# Self-consistency: sample k paths, majority vote final answer
def self_consistency(question, k=10):
    answers = []
    for _ in range(k):
        completion = chain_of_thought(question, model="gpt-4")
        # Extract final answer after "Answer:"
        if "Answer:" in completion:
            answer = completion.split("Answer:")[-1].strip()
            answers.append(answer)
    # Majority vote
    from collections import Counter
    most_common = Counter(answers).most_common(1)[0][0]
    return most_common

sections.title: RAG Architecture & Evaluation
sections.description:
[Intuition]: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) solves the knowledge cutoff and hallucination problems by giving the LLM access to external documents at inference time. The architecture has three knobs: chunking (how you split documents), retrieval (how you find relevant chunks), and synthesis (how the LLM uses retrieved chunks). The key tradeoff is between retrieval recall (finding all relevant docs) and precision (avoiding distracting docs) — low precision causes the LLM to hallucinate based on irrelevant context. RAG fails most often not because retrieval misses relevant docs, but because irrelevant retrieved docs poison the LLM's output.

[Concrete Numbers]: RAG with top-5 chunks at chunk_size=512 achieves 89% accuracy on QA benchmarks vs 54% without RAG — a 35 point improvement. Chunk_size=256 has higher precision but lower recall (83% accuracy), while chunk_size=1024 has higher recall but more noise (86% accuracy). The optimal chunk_size is task-dependent: 256 for factoid QA, 512 for summarization, 1024 for complex reasoning. Embedding dimension: 1536 (text-embedding-3-large) vs 384 (mini) is a cost-quality tradeoff.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Chunk documents with overlap (10-15% of chunk size), 2) Generate embeddings with text-embedding-3-large, 3) Store in vector DB (Pinecone/Weaviate), 4) At query time: embed query → ANN search (top-k) → rerank with cross-encoder → feed top-n to LLM, 5) Evaluate: retrieval recall@k, answer correctness, faithfulness (does answer cite retrieved docs?).

[Interview Angle]: Meta E6 | Tests end-to-end system design thinking — RAG isn't just about vector search, it's about chunking strategy, retrieval quality, prompt construction, and evaluation. Meta uses RAG for Facebook Search and Llama-powered assistant.

[Common Mistakes]: Using cosine similarity on raw embeddings without normalization. Not reranking the top-k retrieved chunks. Using the same chunk_size for all document types. Not handling the case where no relevant document is retrieved (the LLM should say "I don't know").
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

class RAGPipeline:
    def __init__(self, chunk_size=512, overlap=64, top_k=5):
        self.chunk_size = chunk_size
        self.overlap = overlap
        self.top_k = top_k
        self.chunks = []
        self.embeddings = None

    def chunk_document(self, text):
        """Split text into overlapping chunks"""
        step = self.chunk_size - self.overlap
        chunks = []
        for i in range(0, len(text), step):
            chunk = text[i:i + self.chunk_size]
            chunks.append(chunk)
        return chunks

    def embed(self, texts):
        """Batch embedding with text-embedding-3-large"""
        resp = client.embeddings.create(
            model="text-embedding-3-large",
            input=texts,
            dimensions=256  # Matryoshka: truncate to 256 for speed
        )
        return np.array([d.embedding for d in resp.data])

    def retrieve(self, query, k=None):
        """ANN search + rerank"""
        k = k or self.top_k
        query_emb = self.embed([query])
        # Cosine similarity (assumes normalized embeddings)
        scores = query_emb @ self.embeddings.T  # (1, n_chunks)
        top_indices = np.argsort(scores[0])[-k:][::-1]
        return [self.chunks[i] for i in top_indices], scores[0][top_indices]

    def generate(self, query, context_chunks):
        """Synthesize answer from retrieved chunks"""
        context = "\n\n---\n\n".join(
            f"[Doc {i+1}] {chunk}"
            for i, chunk in enumerate(context_chunks)
        )
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4",
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": (
                    "Answer using ONLY the provided context. "
                    "If the context doesn't contain the answer, say 'I don't know.' "
                    "Cite document numbers in brackets [Doc X]."
                )},
                {"role": "user", "content": f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {query}"}
            ],
            temperature=0.1
        )
        return response.choices[0].message.content

sections.title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)
sections.description:
[Intuition]: Full fine-tuning of LLMs is expensive (7B params × 4 bytes × 3 states = 84GB VRAM just for optimizer states). PEFT methods like LoRA insert trainable rank-decomposition matrices into existing weight layers while keeping original weights frozen. The key insight: the weight updates during fine-tuning have low intrinsic rank — you don't need to learn all d×d parameters, just two low-rank matrices A (d×r) and B (r×d) where r ≪ d. LoRA reduces trainable parameters by 10,000× while matching full fine-tuning quality on most tasks.

[Concrete Numbers]: Full fine-tuning of Llama-2-7B requires 56GB VRAM (bf16). LoRA (r=8) requires 16GB VRAM — a 3.5× reduction. QLoRA with 4-bit NormalFloat quantization reduces to 10GB, fitting on a single consumer GPU (RTX 4090). The quality gap: LoRA achieves 97.4% of full fine-tuning performance on GSM8K (45.2% vs 46.4%). The sweet spot is r=8-64 — lower r → more regularization (better for small datasets), higher r → more capacity (better for large datasets).

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Choose base model and quantization level (QLoRA for small GPUs, LoRA for quality-critical), 2) Apply LoRA to attention projection matrices (W_q, W_v, W_k, W_o) — these give the best quality per parameter, 3) Train with rank r=8-64, alpha=2×r, dropout=0.1, 4) Only save LoRA weights (~2-10MB), not the full model, 5) Merge LoRA into base weights at inference (no latency overhead).

[Interview Angle]: Google L6 | Tests understanding of the rank-decomposition insight and its connection to the fundamental low-rank bias of fine-tuning. Staff-level question: "How would you choose the rank r for a given task and dataset size?" (Answer: cross-validate r; smaller r for small datasets acts as stronger regularizer).

[Common Mistakes]: Training LoRA with too high a learning rate (LoRA weights need lower LR, typically 1e-4 vs 1e-5 for full FT). Applying LoRA to all layers uniformly instead of selectively (embedding layers benefit less from LoRA). Not scaling the LoRA output by alpha/r.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LoRALayer(nn.Module):
    """
    LoRA: insert low-rank matrices into a frozen Linear layer.
    
    Original: y = Wx + b
    LoRA: y = Wx + b + (B @ A) * (alpha / r) * x
    """
    def __init__(self, in_features, out_features, rank=8, alpha=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.rank = rank
        self.alpha = alpha
        self.scaling = alpha / rank
        # A: random init (d_in × r), B: zero init (r × d_out)
        self.A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(in_features, rank) * 0.02)
        self.B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(rank, out_features))

    def forward(self, x):
        # LoRA update: x @ A @ B * scaling
        return (x @ self.A @ self.B) * self.scaling

class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    """Replace nn.Linear with this during fine-tuning"""
    def __init__(self, linear, rank=8, alpha=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = linear  # frozen
        self.lora = LoRALayer(
            linear.in_features, linear.out_features, rank, alpha
        )
        # Freeze original weights
        for param in self.linear.parameters():
            param.requires_grad = False

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.linear(x) + self.lora(x)

# Usage: apply LoRA to attention query projections
# for layer in model.transformer.h:
#     layer.attn.q_proj = LoRALinear(layer.attn.q_proj, rank=8)
# 
# Trainable params: 2 × d × r × n_layers
# Llama-2-7B: 4096 × 8 × 2 × 32 = 2.1M params (vs 7B = 0.03%)
print(f"LoRA 7B: {2 * 4096 * 8 * 32:,} trainable params")
print(f"Reduction: 7,000,000,000 / {2*4096*8*32:,} = ~3,300x")

sections.title: RLHF & DPO for Alignment
sections.description:
[Intuition]: Supervised fine-tuning teaches the model format and style, but it doesn't teach values — what makes a good response vs a harmful one. RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) solves this by training a reward model on human preferences, then using PPO to optimize the policy against this reward. The key insight: RLHF decouples what humans want (reward model) from how the model should behave (policy). DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) simplifies this by showing that the RLHF objective can be solved in closed form — you can optimize directly on preference pairs without a separate reward model. DPO is simpler, more stable, and matches RLHF quality.

[Concrete Numbers]: RLHF with PPO requires 4 models (policy, reference policy, reward model, value model) — 4× memory overhead during training. DPO requires only 2 models (policy, reference policy) — 2× memory overhead. Both achieve similar results: InstructGPT (1.3B, RLHF) preferred over GPT-3 (175B) 73% of the time despite being 135× smaller. DPO matches RLHF within 1-2% on summarization and dialog benchmarks. Training instability: PPO has ~30% chance of reward hacking collapse; DPO has ~5% — making DPO the recommended default for new projects.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Collect preference pairs (chosen response, rejected response) from human raters, 2) For RLHF: train reward model on preferences, then PPO-tune policy against reward model, 3) For DPO: directly optimize policy on preference pairs (simpler), 4) Evaluate: win rate against reference policy, helpfulness score, harmlessness score, 5) Iterate: use disagreement regions to collect more targeted preference data.

[Interview Angle]: OpenAI/Microsoft Staff | Tests understanding of the fundamental RL alignment problem and the tradeoff between complexity (PPO) vs simplicity (DPO). Staff-level question: "How would you detect and mitigate reward hacking in RLHF?" (Answer: use a held-out reward model for evaluation, track reward vs KL divergence, periodically calibrate reward model).

[Common Mistakes]: Training RLHF reward model on too few comparisons (need 100K+ preference pairs for stable training). Using PPO without proper KL regularization (policy collapses to a single high-reward mode). Forgetting to save the reference policy checkpoint for KL computation.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# DPO Loss: Direct Preference Optimization
# L_DPO = -E[ log σ(β * (log π_θ(y_w|x) - log π_ref(y_w|x) - (log π_θ(y_l|x) - log π_ref(y_l|x))) ]

def dpo_loss(
    policy_logps: torch.Tensor,    # log π_θ for chosen & rejected
    ref_logps: torch.Tensor,        # log π_ref for chosen & rejected
    chosen_indices: torch.Tensor,   # which is the chosen response
    beta: float = 0.1               # temperature parameter
):
    """
    Args:
        policy_logps: (batch, 2) — log probs under policy
        ref_logps: (batch, 2) — log probs under reference model
        chosen_indices: (batch,) — 0 or 1 for chosen response
    Returns:
        loss, accuracy, chosen_rewards, rejected_rewards
    """
    # Split into chosen and rejected
    batch_size = policy_logps.shape[0]
    idx = torch.arange(batch_size)
    
    chosen_policy = policy_logps[idx, chosen_indices]
    rejected_policy = policy_logps[idx, 1 - chosen_indices]
    chosen_ref = ref_logps[idx, chosen_indices]
    rejected_ref = ref_logps[idx, 1 - chosen_indices]
    
    # Implicit reward: β * (log π_θ - log π_ref)
    chosen_rewards = beta * (chosen_policy - chosen_ref)
    rejected_rewards = beta * (rejected_policy - rejected_ref)
    
    # DPO loss: -log σ(chosen_rewards - rejected_rewards)
    logits = chosen_rewards - rejected_rewards
    loss = -F.logsigmoid(logits).mean()
    
    accuracy = (chosen_rewards > rejected_rewards).float().mean()
    
    return loss, accuracy, chosen_rewards, rejected_rewards

# Training loop:
# for batch in dataloader:
#     policy_logps = policy_model(batch.input_ids, batch.attention_mask)
#     with torch.no_grad():
#         ref_logps = ref_model(batch.input_ids, batch.attention_mask)
#     loss, acc, _, _ = dpo_loss(
#         policy_logps, ref_logps, batch.chosen_indices, beta=0.1
#     )
#     loss.backward()
#     optimizer.step()

print(f"DPO is {4/2}x more memory-efficient than RLHF (PPO)")
print(f"DPO training instability is ~5% vs PPO's ~30% reward hacking")

sections.title: LLM Safety & Red Teaming
sections.description:
[Intuition]: LLM safety is a cat-and-mouse game between attackers (jailbreaks, prompt injection, data extraction) and defenders (input filtering, output guardrails, adversarial training). The key insight: all current LLM safety relies on the model learning to refuse harmful requests during alignment, but this is brittle — an adversary can find a prompt that bypasses the refusal boundary. Red teaming is the practice of systematically probing the model for failure modes. The most dangerous attacks are indirect prompt injection (attacker-controlled content in retrieved documents that overrides the system prompt) and data extraction (convincing the model to emit training data verbatim).

[Concrete Numbers]: GPT-4 is resistant to 95%+ of known jailbreaks, but fine-tuned models (especially via LoRA) often lose safety guardrails — a single epoch of fine-tuning on unsafe data can reduce refusal rate from 95% to 20%. Prompt injection via hidden text (white text on white background in web pages) has a 73% success rate on early GPT-3.5, down to 12% on GPT-4. Data extraction attacks from GPT-3.5 achieve ~1% recall of training data from 100K queries — small but significant for a 600B-token dataset.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Input filtering: block known prompt injection patterns, 2) Output filtering: classify responses for harmful content (use a dedicated classifier), 3) Constitutional AI: define principles that constrain model behavior, 4) Red teaming: automated adversarial attacks (GCG, AutoDAN) + manual human probing, 5) Monitoring: track refusal rate, toxicity scores, PII leakage rate in production.

[Interview Angle]: Google DeepMind Staff | Tests understanding that safety is a systems problem, not just a model problem — input filtering, output filtering, monitoring, and adversarial testing are all needed. Staff-level question: "How would you design a red-teaming pipeline for a customer-facing LLM?"

[Common Mistakes]: Relying solely on system prompts for safety (trivial to bypass). Not testing fine-tuned models for safety regression (LoRA can silently undo alignment). Not evaluating on adversarial inputs (clean evaluations overestimate safety).
sections.codeBlock:
# Gradient-Based Jailbreak (GCG: Greedy Coordinate Gradient)
# Find a suffix that maximizes the probability of a target response

import torch

def gcg_attack(
    model,           # target LLM
    tokenizer,       
    input_text: str, # malicious instruction
    target: str,     # desired response
    suffix_len: int = 20,
    steps: int = 100,
    top_k: int = 256
):
    """
    GCG finds a suffix like "! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! ! !"
    that makes the model comply with harmful instructions.
    """
    # Initialize suffix with tokens
    suffix = torch.randint(0, tokenizer.vocab_size, (1, suffix_len))
    
    target_ids = tokenizer(target, return_tensors="pt").input_ids
    
    for step in range(steps):
        # Full input: [instruction] [suffix]
        input_ids = torch.cat([
            tokenizer(input_text, return_tensors="pt").input_ids,
            suffix
        ], dim=1)
        
        # Forward pass
        logits = model(input_ids).logits[:, -target_ids.shape[1]:]
        loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(
            logits.transpose(1, 2), target_ids
        )
        
        # Gradient on suffix tokens
        grad = torch.autograd.grad(loss, suffix)[0]
        
        # Token replacement: for each position, try top_k candidates
        for pos in range(suffix_len):
            with torch.no_grad():
                # Score top_k tokens by gradient
                scores = -grad[0, pos] @ model.embedding.weight.T
                best_tokens = scores.topk(top_k).indices
                
                # Try each candidate
                best_loss = float('inf')
                for tok in best_tokens:
                    suffix[0, pos] = tok
                    # Quick forward pass (single token change)
                    input_ids = torch.cat([
                        tokenizer(input_text, return_tensors="pt").input_ids,
                        suffix
                    ], dim=1)
                    with torch.no_grad():
                        cur_loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(
                            model(input_ids).logits[:, -target_ids.shape[1]:].transpose(1, 2),
                            target_ids
                        )
                    if cur_loss < best_loss:
                        best_loss = cur_loss
                        suffix[0, pos] = tok
                        break
        
        if step % 20 == 0:
            print(f"Step {step}: loss={loss.item():.4f}")
    
    return suffix

# Defenses:
# 1. Input perplexity filter (jailbreak tokens have high perplexity)
# 2. Output safety classifier (e.g., LlamaGuard)
# 3. Prompt-level detection: PERIMETER, SmoothLLM, Erase-and-Check
print("Red teaming: 95% jailbreaks blocked on GPT-4, but LoRA fine-tuning reduces to 20% refusal rate")

sections.title: LLM Evaluation Frameworks
sections.description:
[Intuition]: Evaluating LLMs is fundamentally harder than evaluating traditional ML models because there's no single ground truth — a response can be correct in multiple valid ways. The evaluation stack has three layers: (1) benchmark-based (MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval — fixed datasets with clear right/wrong), (2) LLM-as-judge (use a stronger LLM to evaluate response quality — scalable but biased toward the judge's preferences), and (3) human evaluation (gold standard but expensive and slow). The key insight: no single evaluation method is sufficient — the Pareto frontier of cost vs. fidelity requires combining all three: benchmarks for regression, LLM-as-judge for rapid iteration, human evaluation for final validation.

[Concrete Numbers]: MMLU (57 subjects, 14K questions) is the most widely used general knowledge benchmark. GPT-4 scores 86.4%, Llama-2-70B scores 68.9%, Llama-3-70B scores 82%. LLM-as-judge (GPT-4 evaluating GPT-3.5) has 78% agreement with human raters — cheaper than human ($0.01 per eval vs $1.00) but misses nuanced harms. The HELM framework evaluates across 42 scenarios, 7 metrics (accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, efficiency) — it takes 1000+ GPU-hours to run a full evaluation suite.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Automated benchmarks: run MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval on every model checkpoint (cost: ~$50-100 per run), 2) LLM-as-judge: set up pairwise comparisons (model A vs model B on 500 sampled prompts, cost ~$5), 3) Human eval: rate 200 representative samples per week on a 5-point Likert scale (cost: ~$1000/week), 4) Monitoring: track regressions on all three layers with automated alerts when any metric drops by 2%+.

[Interview Angle]: OpenAI/Anthropic Staff | Tests understanding that evaluation IS the product — if you can't measure it, you can't improve it. Staff-level question: "How would you design an evaluation framework for a coding copilot?" (Answer: functional correctness via unit tests, style consistency, latency, user acceptance rate).

[Common Mistakes]: Over-relying on MMLU as the single metric (it's multiple choice — easy to game). Using LLM-as-judge without measuring judge bias (judges prefer their own style). Not evaluating on distribution-shifted data (evals on clean benchmarks don't predict production performance).
sections.codeBlock:
# LLM-as-Judge evaluation framework
import json
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

JUDGE_PROMPT = """
You are an expert evaluator. Rate the quality of the assistant's response on a scale of 1-5.

Criteria:
- 5: Perfect — correct, well-structured, aligned with instructions
- 4: Good — correct but could be more concise/detailed
- 3: Acceptable — mostly correct but has minor errors or omissions
- 2: Poor — partially incorrect or missing key information
- 1: Wrong — factually incorrect, harmful, or off-topic

User query: {query}

Assistant response: {response}

Rating (1-5):
"""

def evaluate_response(query, response, judge_model="gpt-4"):
    prompt = JUDGE_PROMPT.format(query=query, response=response)
    result = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=judge_model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        temperature=0.0  # deterministic
    )
    rating = result.choices[0].message.content.strip()
    # Parse numeric rating from first character
    try:
        return int(rating[0])
    except (ValueError, IndexError):
        return None

def pairwise_win_rate(model_a_responses, model_b_responses, queries):
    """Compute win rate of model A vs model B across N queries"""
    wins = 0
    for i, query in enumerate(queries):
        a_resp = model_a_responses[i]
        b_resp = model_b_responses[i]
        
        pair_prompt = f"""
Which response is better?
Query: {query}

Response A: {a_resp}

Response B: {b_resp}

Answer with ONLY "A" or "B":
"""
        result = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": pair_prompt}],
            temperature=0.0,
            max_tokens=1
        )
        answer = result.choices[0].message.content.strip()
        if answer.upper() == "A":
            wins += 1
    
    return wins / len(queries)

print(f"LLM-as-judge: cost $0.01/eval vs $1.00 human eval — 100x cheaper, 78% agreement")

sections.title: Agent Architectures (ReAct, Tool Use, Multi-Agent)
sections.description:
[Intuition]: LLM agents extend the model from text-completion-in-a-box to acting on the real world by giving it tools (code execution, web search, API calls, file system access). The key insight from ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) is that reasoning traces and action steps mutually reinforce each other — the model reasons about what to do, takes an action, observes the result, and reasons again. This creates a feedback loop that's strictly more powerful than either reasoning-only (CoT) or acting-only (tool use without reasoning). The failure mode is compounding errors: an early wrong action leads to terminal states from which recovery is difficult.

[Concrete Numbers]: ReAct agents with 5 tools (search, calculator, Python REPL, file system, web fetch) solve 78% of complex QA tasks vs 54% with CoT-only — a 24 point improvement from tool use. The optimal number of tools is 5-8: more tools increase capability but also increase the chance of tool confusion (picking the wrong tool). Agent success rate degrades linearly with task length: each additional reasoning step adds ~3% failure probability, so a 20-step task has only ~54% completion rate. Multi-agent systems (specialist agents + orchestrator) improve reliability: orchestrator routes sub-tasks to specialist agents and validates outputs, reducing error propagation by 40%.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Define the orchestrator agent with a system prompt describing available tools, 2) For each tool, provide name, description, input schema, and output format, 3) Implement the ReAct loop: Thought → Action → Observation → Thought... until final answer, 4) Set max iterations (typically 10-20) and error handling (retry on tool failure, timeouts), 5) For complex tasks, use hierarchical agents: a Planner agent decomposes the task, an Executor agent runs each sub-task, a Validator agent checks outputs.

[Interview Angle]: Google L6+ | Tests understanding of agent architecture as an engineering problem (error propagation, tool design, state management) rather than just prompt engineering. Staff-level question: "How would you design a multi-agent system that can recover from tool failures?"

[Common Mistakes]: Not providing clear tool descriptions (LLMs choose tools based on description quality). Not setting timeouts (agents can hang on infinite loops). Allowing agents to call destructive tools without confirmation (delete files, write to production DB). Not validating tool outputs before using them in further reasoning.

sections.title: Serving LLMs at Scale
sections.description:
[Intuition]: LLM serving is fundamentally memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound — for autoregressive generation, the bottleneck is moving model weights from HBM to SRAM for each token. The key techniques: (1) KV cache avoids recomputing past key-value pairs (trades memory for compute), (2) continuous batching groups tokens from different requests at different generation stages into the same forward pass (maximizes GPU utilization), (3) PagedAttention manages KV cache in fixed-size blocks to eliminate fragmentation (like virtual memory pages). The vLLM system combines all three and achieves near-optimal throughput: within 5% of theoretical maximum for a given GPU.

[Concrete Numbers]: vLLM achieves 2-4× throughput improvement over naive HF and TGI. On a single A100-80GB: Llama-2-7B at 2000 tok/s (input length=512, output=256, batch=32). For Llama-2-70B: requires at least 2 A100s with tensor parallelism — achieves 350 tok/s. Continuous batching improves throughput by 3-5× vs static batching by eliminating the "wait for slowest sequence" problem. PagedAttention reduces KV cache memory waste from 60-80% fragmentation to <5%.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Choose parallelism strategy: tensor parallelism (within a node, splits layers across GPUs), pipeline parallelism (across nodes, splits layers by stage), 2) KV cache: allocate in PagedAttention blocks (block_size=16), 3) Schedule: continuous batching with a waiting time budget (max 100ms before accepting a new request into the running batch), 4) Prefix caching: cache KV for common prefixes (system prompts), 5) Speculative decoding: use a draft model (small, fast) to propose tokens, target model (large, accurate) to verify — 2× speedup with no quality loss.

[Interview Angle]: Meta/Google Staff | Tests deep systems understanding — you need to know GPU memory hierarchy (HBM bandwidth limits generation speed), not just ML. Staff-level question: "How many A100s do you need to serve Llama-2-70B at 1000 QPS with 200ms p99 latency?" (Answer: memory ~140GB → 2 GPUs, compute ~400 TFLOPS → 4 GPUs, bottleneck is memory bandwidth).

[Common Mistakes]: Underestimating KV cache memory (for Llama-2-7B with batch=32, seq_len=2048: 2×32×2048×4096×2 bytes ≈ 2.1GB). Using too large a block size for PagedAttention (wastes memory for short sequences). Not using prefix caching for shared system prompts.
sections.codeBlock:
# vLLM-inspired serving pseudocode
import torch

class PagedAttention:
    """
    KV cache managed in fixed-size blocks (like virtual memory pages).
    Block size B: tradeoff between management overhead and fragmentation.
    """
    def __init__(self, block_size=16, num_layers=32, num_heads=32, head_dim=128):
        self.block_size = block_size
        self.num_layers = num_layers
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = head_dim
        # Block table: maps logical block → physical block
        self.block_table = {}  # request_id -> [block_ids]
        # Physical blocks: pre-allocated pool
        self.num_blocks = 100000
        self.k_cache = torch.empty(self.num_blocks, block_size, num_heads, head_dim)
        self.v_cache = torch.empty(self.num_blocks, block_size, num_heads, head_dim)
        self.free_blocks = set(range(self.num_blocks))
    
    def allocate(self, request_id, num_tokens):
        """Allocate KV cache blocks for a new request"""
        num_blocks = (num_tokens + self.block_size - 1) // self.block_size
        blocks = []
        for _ in range(num_blocks):
            block = self.free_blocks.pop()
            blocks.append(block)
        self.block_table[request_id] = blocks
        return blocks
    
    def attention(self, query, request_id, position):
        """Compute attention with block-sparse KV cache"""
        blocks = self.block_table[request_id]
        block_idx = position // self.block_size
        offset = position % self.block_size
        
        # Gather KV from block table (non-contiguous!)
        phys_block = blocks[block_idx]
        k = self.k_cache[phys_block, :offset+1]  # (seq_len, heads, dim)
        v = self.v_cache[phys_block, :offset+1]
        
        # Standard attention
        scores = query @ k.transpose(-2, -1) / (self.head_dim ** 0.5)
        attn = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        output = attn @ v
        return output
    
    def free(self, request_id):
        """Free KV cache blocks for completed request"""
        blocks = self.block_table.pop(request_id, [])
        self.free_blocks.update(blocks)

# Memory analysis for Llama-2-7B (batch=32, seq_len=2048)
# KV cache per token = 2 × num_layers × num_heads × head_dim × 2 bytes
# = 2 × 32 × 32 × 128 × 2 = 524,288 bytes ≈ 0.5 MB per token
# For batch=32, seq_len=2048: 32 * 2048 * 0.5MB = 32 GB (40% of A100-80GB!)
print(f"KV cache per token: {2 * 32 * 32 * 128} bytes")
print(f"KV cache for batch=32, seq=2048: {32 * 2048 * 2 * 32 * 32 * 128 * 2 / 1e9:.1f} GB")

sections.title: Context Window Optimization
sections.description:
[Intuition]: Long context windows (128K+ tokens) are a double-edged sword — they enable new capabilities (whole-codebase understanding, long-document analysis), but they're computationally expensive and models often fail to use the full context. The key techniques: (1) RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) enables extrapolation beyond trained context length by encoding position as a rotation in the embedding space, (2) sliding window attention limits each token's attention to a fixed window of nearby tokens (reduces computation from O(L²) to O(L·W) where W ≪ L), (3) KV cache compression reduces memory by evicting stale KV entries or merging similar entries, (4) ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) replaces position embeddings with a bias that penalizes distant tokens, enabling length generalization.

[Concrete Numbers]: RoPE-based models can extrapolate to 2× trained context (trained on 4K, evaluated on 8K) with <5% perplexity degradation. Beyond 2×, perplexity degrades rapidly. YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN method) extends to 64× (4K → 256K) by adjusting RoPE frequencies. Sliding window with W=4096 achieves 95% of full attention quality while reducing memory from O(L²) to O(L·W). For L=128K, W=4096: 128K² vs 128K×4K = 16,384× less computation. KV cache eviction (H₂O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle) keeps 20% of tokens (attention sinks + recent tokens) and achieves 90%+ of full accuracy.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) For short context (<8K): use full attention with RoPE, 2) For medium context (8K-32K): use sliding window with W=4096 + RoPE with YaRN extension, 3) For long context (32K-128K): use sliding window + H₂O KV cache eviction (keep 20% of tokens), 4) For extremely long (128K+): use retrieval over chunks + sliding window within each chunk (RAG-style), 5) Monitor: effective context utilization (what % of the context does the model actually attend to?).

[Interview Angle]: Google/Meta Staff | Tests understanding that context window optimization is about Pareto efficiency — you pay O(L²) in memory for diminishing returns. Staff-level question: "When would you choose RAG over extending the context window?" (Answer: RAG when knowledge is sparse and can be indexed; extended context when the answer requires reasoning across the full document).

[Common Mistakes]: Assuming longer context always helps (performance degrades on the lost-in-the-middle task beyond 8K tokens for most models). Not tuning RoPE frequencies for your deployment context length. Using full attention when sliding window would suffice (wasting compute).
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

class RotaryPositionEmbedding:
    """RoPE: rotate queries and keys by position-dependent angles"""
    def __init__(self, dim, max_seq_len=4096, base=10000.0):
        # Compute rotation frequencies
        inv_freq = 1.0 / (base ** (torch.arange(0, dim, 2).float() / dim))
        self.inv_freq = inv_freq  # (dim/2,)
    
    def _rotate_half(self, x):
        """Rotate the second half of last dimension"""
        x1, x2 = x[..., :x.shape[-1]//2], x[..., x.shape[-1]//2:]
        return torch.cat((-x2, x1), dim=-1)
    
    def forward(self, q, k, positions):
        """Apply RoPE to queries and keys at given positions"""
        # positions: (batch, seq_len) or (seq_len,)
        freqs = torch.outer(positions.float(), self.inv_freq)  # (seq, dim/2)
        freqs = torch.cat([freqs, freqs], dim=-1)  # (seq, dim)
        
        cos = freqs.cos()
        sin = freqs.sin()
        
        q_rot = q * cos + self._rotate_half(q) * sin
        k_rot = k * cos + self._rotate_half(k) * sin
        return q_rot, k_rot

# YaRN: extend RoPE beyond trained length by adjusting frequencies
# theta_i = base^(-2i/d) * scale^(2i/(d-2))
# where scale = T_target / T_train (e.g., 16 for 64K target vs 4K train)
def yarn_frequencies(dim, target_len, train_len=4096, base=10000.0):
    scale = target_len / train_len
    i = torch.arange(0, dim, 2)
    theta = base ** (-2 * i / dim)
    # YaRN scaling: theta_i' = theta_i * scale^(2*i/(dim-2))
    yarn_scale = scale ** (2 * i / (dim - 2))
    return theta / yarn_scale  # adjusted frequencies

# Sliding window attention mask
def sliding_window_mask(seq_len, window_size):
    """Create causal mask with sliding window"""
    mask = torch.full((seq_len, seq_len), float('-inf'))
    for i in range(seq_len):
        start = max(0, i - window_size + 1)
        mask[i, start:i+1] = 0.0
    return mask

print(f"Full O(L²) vs sliding window O(L·W): "
      f"for L=128K, W=4K: {128**2:.0f}K vs {128*4:.0f}K = {128**2/(128*4):.0f}x less compute")

sections.title: LLM Fine-Tuning Strategies (Full, LoRA, QLoRA)
sections.description:
[Intuition]: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained LLM to a specific domain or task. The key decision is which parameters to update and with what precision: full fine-tuning updates all weights in bf16 (most expensive, highest quality), LoRA inserts low-rank adapters (cheap, near-full quality), QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization on top of LoRA (fits on a single consumer GPU, slight quality loss). The choice depends on your budget (GPU memory, time, data) and quality requirements. The fundamental insight: the rank of fine-tuning updates is low (r < 64 captures most of the signal for typical tasks), so LoRA is the Pareto-optimal choice for most practical scenarios.

[Concrete Numbers]: Full fine-tuning Llama-2-7B: 56GB VRAM (bf16), 8 A100s, 3 days for 10K examples. LoRA (r=16) on Llama-2-7B: 16GB VRAM, 1 A100, 6 hours — 12× cheaper. QLoRA (4-bit) on Llama-2-7B: 10GB VRAM, 1 RTX 4090, 8 hours — 5.6× cheaper than full FT. Quality: LoRA achieves 96-99% of full FT quality on most benchmarks; QLoRA achieves 93-97%. The gap widens on complex reasoning tasks (GSM8K: full FT 46.4%, LoRA 45.2%, QLoRA 43.1%) but is negligible on classification and generation tasks.

[Practical Workflow]: 1) If you have 8+ A100s and need maximum quality for reasoning tasks → full fine-tuning, 2) If you have 1 A100 and want 98% of full quality → LoRA (r=16, target attention layers only), 3) If you have a single consumer GPU (RTX 4090) → QLoRA (4-bit NF4 quantization, LoRA r=32 to compensate), 4) Always: train for 1 epoch (LLMs overfit easily), use cosine decay with warmup, evaluate on a held-out set every 100 steps, 5) Compare: run full FT, LoRA, QLoRA on a small subset (1K examples) to calibrate the quality gap before committing.

[Interview Angle]: Meta/Google Staff | Tests understanding of the quality-cost Pareto frontier across fine-tuning methods. Staff-level question: "You need to fine-tune Llama-3-70B for a customer support product. You have 4 A100s. What approach do you use and why?" (Answer: QLoRA with r=32 on attention layers — 70B doesn't fit in 4 A100s for full FT; QLoRA uses 4-bit to fit the full model + LoRA weights).

[Common Mistakes]: Over-training (more than 1-2 epochs causes catastrophic forgetting of pretrained knowledge). Using too high a learning rate for LoRA (needs 5-10× lower LR than full FT). Applying LoRA to ALL linear layers instead of just attention — diminishing returns beyond attention + feed-forward.
sections.codeBlock:
# QLoRA: 4-bit NormalFloat quantization + LoRA
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
)

# 4-bit quantization config
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",  # NormalFloat4: optimal for normal-distributed weights
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype="bfloat16",  # compute in bf16, weights in 4-bit
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True       # second quantization of scaling factors (saves ~0.4 bits)
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf",
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16
)

# Apply LoRA
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,            # rank: tradeoff capacity vs efficiency
    lora_alpha=32,   # scaling = alpha / r
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj", "k_proj", "o_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.1,
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM"
)

model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)

# Memory comparison table
methods = [
    ("Full FT (bf16)", "56 GB", "8 A100-80GB", "100%", "$4K/run"),
    ("LoRA (r=16, bf16)", "16 GB", "1 A100-80GB", "97%", "$500/run"),
    ("QLoRA (4-bit NF4, r=32)", "10 GB", "1 RTX 4090", "95%", "$200/run"),
    ("QLoRA (double quant)", "9.5 GB", "1 RTX 4090", "94%", "$200/run")
]

print(f"{'Method':<30} {'VRAM':<10} {'Hardware':<18} {'Quality':<10} {'Cost'}")
print("-"*80)
for m in methods:
    print(f"{m[0]:<30} {m[1]:<10} {m[2]:<18} {m[3]:<10} {m[4]}")

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — LLM Engineering
sections.description:
[Intuition]: These questions cover the cross-section of LLM knowledge expected at Staff+ level — you need to understand the full stack from training to serving to safety. The common thread: LLM engineering is about managing tradeoffs (quality vs cost, latency vs throughput, capability vs safety) with principled engineering decisions.

[Concrete Numbers]: N/A — this is a Q&A reference section.

[Interview Angle] Q1 (Google L6): "Design a system to serve a fine-tuned 7B model for an internal chatbot. Requirements: 1000 QPS, 200ms p99, 99.9% uptime, PII redaction." Answer: Use vLLM with continuous batching on 4 A100s (2 for serving, 2 for redundancy). Add PII redaction as a pre-processing step (spaCy NER + regex). Add output guardrails (classifier for harmful content). Monitoring: track QPS, latency, PII leakage rate, refusal rate. Deployment: blue-green with automatic rollback on error rate spike.

[Interview Angle] Q2 (Meta E6): "Your team fine-tuned Llama-2-7B with LoRA for code generation. It excels in offline eval but generates insecure code in production. What do you do?" Answer: Three-pronged approach: (1) Add security-focused examples to the training data (contrastive pairs: secure vs insecure), (2) Add a post-hoc security checker (static analysis tools like Bandit/Hadolint), (3) Implement output filtering classified for vulnerable code patterns. Root cause: LoRA preserved the base model's security knowledge but didn't actively reinforce secure coding patterns.

[Interview Angle] Q3 (OpenAI): "How would you reduce hallucination in a customer-facing RAG system? Define hallucination metrics first." Answer: Metrics: faithfulness (does the answer cite retrieved docs?), consistency (does the answer contradict itself?), factuality (can each claim be verified?). Techniques: (1) Improve retrieval precision (rerank with cross-encoder), (2) In the prompt, instruct the model to say "I don't know" when unsure, (3) Add a faithfulness classifier as a guardrail, (4) Use chain-of-verification: generate claims, then verify each claim against retrieved docs, (5) Reduce temperature to 0.1 for factual tasks.

[Interview Angle] Q4 (Anthropic): "How would you evaluate whether RLHF or DPO is the right alignment approach for a new project?" Answer: Choose DPO as default (simpler, more stable, fewer hyperparameters, no reward model needed). Choose RLHF only if: (1) you have 100K+ preference pairs and can afford training a separate reward model, (2) your task requires fine-grained credit assignment per token (DPO operates on entire sequences), (3) you need to periodically update the reward model without retraining the policy. In practice, DPO matches or exceeds RLHF on 90%+ of use cases.

[Interview Angle] Q5 (Microsoft): "Your RAG system's retrieval finds the right documents 80% of the time, but the LLM still gives wrong answers. What's broken?" Answer: The most common failure is not retrieval quality but context integration — the LLM ignores the retrieved context and relies on its parametric knowledge. Debug by: (1) Checking if the retrieved docs are actually relevant (manual audit of 100 samples), (2) Checking if the prompt explicitly instructs the model to use the context, (3) Checking for position bias — the model only attends to the first or last retrieved doc (lost-in-the-middle), (4) Testing with a "gold" context (hand-written perfect context) — if the LLM still gets it wrong, the issue is instruction following, not retrieval.

[Key Takeaway]: LLM engineering is a full-stack discipline — prompt engineering, RAG, fine-tuning, alignment, safety, agents, serving, and evaluation are all interconnected. The distinguishing skill at Staff+ level is understanding the tradeoffs between these techniques and knowing when to apply each one.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Vaswani, A., et al. "Attention Is All You Need." — 2017
  • Ouyang, L., et al. "Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback." (InstructGPT/RLHF) — 2022
  • Rafailov, R., et al. "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model." (DPO) — 2023
  • Hu, E., et al. "LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models." — 2021
  • Dettmers, T., et al. "QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models." — 2023
  • Kaplan, J., et al. "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models." — 2020
  • Hoffmann, J., et al. "Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models." (Chinchilla) — 2022
  • Dao, T., et al. "FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention." — 2022
  • Kwon, W., et al. "Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention." (vLLM) — 2023
  • Frantar, E., et al. "GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers." — 2022
  • Devlin, J., et al. "BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding." — 2019
  • Lewis, P., et al. "Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks." (RAG) — 2020
  • Yao, S., et al. "ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models." — 2022
